s: The first exoplanet orbiting a Sun-like star, 51 Pegasi b, was discovered in 1995 — it's a 'hot Jupiter' that orbits its star in just 4 days.
q: In what year was the first exoplanet orbiting a Sun-like star discovered?
e: This discovery by Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz (Nobel Prize 2019) revolutionized astronomy. The planet is so close to its star that its surface temperature exceeds 1,000°C.

s: Titan, Saturn's largest moon, is the only moon in the solar system with a thick atmosphere — and it has lakes and rivers of liquid methane and ethane.
q: What liquid fills the lakes and rivers on Saturn's moon Titan?
e: Titan's atmosphere is denser than Earth's, and the Huygens probe landed there in 2005, revealing an alien landscape with hydrocarbon rain, rivers, and seas under an orange hazy sky.

s: The Andromeda Galaxy is heading toward the Milky Way at about 110 km/s and the two galaxies will collide in roughly 4.5 billion years.
q: In approximately how many years will the Andromeda Galaxy collide with the Milky Way?
e: When they merge, surprisingly few stars will actually collide — the space between stars is so vast. The two galaxies will eventually form a new giant elliptical galaxy nicknamed 'Milkomeda.'

s: The Parker Solar Probe is the fastest human-made object ever, reaching speeds over 635,000 km/h as it swoops close to the Sun.
q: What is the fastest human-made object ever built?
e: At its closest approach, Parker flies within 6.2 million km of the Sun's surface — closer than any spacecraft before. Its heat shield endures temperatures of nearly 1,400°C while keeping instruments at room temperature.

s: There is a massive cloud of alcohol in space — Sagittarius B2 contains enough ethyl alcohol to fill 400 trillion trillion pints of beer.
q: What unusual substance is found in enormous quantities in the molecular cloud Sagittarius B2?
e: This molecular cloud near the galactic center is one of the largest known, spanning 150 light-years. It contains various organic molecules including ethanol, the same alcohol found in drinks.

s: Binary star systems are more common than single stars — over half of all star systems in our galaxy contain two or more stars orbiting each other.
q: What fraction of star systems in our galaxy are binary or multiple star systems?
e: Our Sun is actually unusual for being alone. In many binary systems, the stars orbit so closely they can exchange material, sometimes leading to spectacular nova explosions.

s: The Boomerang Nebula is the coldest known natural place in the universe at -272°C, just 1 degree above absolute zero.
q: What is the coldest known natural place in the universe?
e: This pre-planetary nebula is colder than the cosmic microwave background because its rapidly expanding gas cools adiabatically — like how a spray can feels cold when you release the pressure.

s: Olympus Mons on Mars is the tallest known volcano in the solar system at 21.9 km high — nearly 2.5 times the height of Mount Everest.
q: What is the tallest volcano in the solar system?
e: This shield volcano is so enormous that its base would cover most of France. Its slope is so gentle that standing on its edge, you couldn't tell you were on a mountain — the base would extend beyond the horizon.

s: The TRAPPIST-1 system has seven Earth-sized planets orbiting a cool red dwarf star, with three in the habitable zone where liquid water could exist.
q: How many Earth-sized planets orbit the star TRAPPIST-1?
e: This system 40 light-years away is remarkably compact — all seven planets orbit closer than Mercury does to our Sun, and neighboring planets would appear larger than the Moon in each other's skies.

s: When two neutron stars collide, they create a kilonova that forges heavy elements like gold and platinum — most gold on Earth likely came from such events.
q: Where is most of Earth's gold believed to have originally been created?
e: The 2017 detection of gravitational waves from a neutron star merger (GW170817) confirmed this theory. A single kilonova can produce several Earth-masses worth of gold.

s: Space is not a perfect vacuum — there are about 1-2 atoms of hydrogen per cubic centimeter in interstellar space.
q: How many atoms are typically found per cubic centimeter in interstellar space?
e: While incredibly sparse compared to Earth's atmosphere (which has about 25 quintillion molecules per cm³), this thin gas makes up a significant fraction of normal matter in the universe.

s: The Crab Nebula is the remnant of a supernova explosion recorded by Chinese astronomers in 1054 AD — the 'guest star' was visible in daylight for 23 days.
q: In what year was the supernova that created the Crab Nebula recorded?
e: At its center spins a pulsar rotating 30 times per second, powering the nebula's glow. This was one of the first objects linked to a historical record of a supernova.

s: Io, Jupiter's innermost large moon, is the most volcanically active body in the solar system with over 400 active volcanoes.
q: Which body in the solar system is the most volcanically active?
e: Tidal forces from Jupiter's immense gravity constantly squeeze and stretch Io, generating internal heat. Some of its volcanic plumes shoot material 500 km above the surface.

s: The observable universe has a diameter of about 93 billion light-years, even though it's only 13.8 billion years old.
q: What is the diameter of the observable universe?
e: This seeming paradox occurs because space itself has been expanding since the Big Bang. Light from the most distant objects started traveling when they were much closer, but space stretched during the journey.

s: Solar flares can release energy equivalent to millions of 100-megaton hydrogen bombs in just a few minutes.
q: What effect can powerful solar flares have on Earth?
a: Disrupt GPS, communications, and power grids
d: Increase atmospheric oxygen levels in the upper ionosphere
Redirect solar wind directly toward Earth's magnetic poles
Cause permanent damage to Earth's magnetic field core
Accelerate the Moon's escape from Earth's orbit
Trigger volcanic eruptions in subduction zones
Increase surface water temperatures by several degrees
e: These sudden eruptions on the Sun's surface accelerate particles to near light speed. The most powerful ones can disrupt GPS, communications, and power grids on Earth — the 1859 Carrington Event caused telegraph wires to spark and catch fire.

s: The Kepler Space Telescope discovered over 2,600 confirmed exoplanets during its mission from 2009 to 2018.
q: How many confirmed exoplanets did the Kepler Space Telescope discover?
e: Kepler revolutionized our understanding of planetary systems by monitoring the brightness of over 150,000 stars, detecting the tiny dips caused by planets passing in front of them (the transit method).

s: If you fell into a stellar-mass black hole, tidal forces would stretch you into a thin strand of atoms — a process scientists actually call 'spaghettification.'
q: What is the scientific term for being stretched apart by a black hole's tidal forces?
e: The gravity difference between your head and feet near a stellar black hole is so extreme that you'd be pulled apart like taffy. Larger supermassive black holes are gentler — you could cross the event horizon without feeling much at first.

s: The Arecibo message, beamed into space in 1974, was a 1,679-bit radio signal aimed at star cluster M13, but it won't arrive for about 25,000 years.
q: When was the Arecibo message transmitted into space?
e: Designed by Frank Drake and Carl Sagan, this binary-coded message contains information about humans, DNA, the solar system, and the Arecibo telescope itself — a deliberate attempt to contact extraterrestrial intelligence.

s: The surface of the Sun is about 5,500°C, but its outer atmosphere (the corona) is over 1 million°C — and scientists still don't fully understand why.
q: What is puzzling about the temperature of the Sun's corona?
a: It's much hotter than the surface below it
d: The corona has less radiation than the photosphere
Solar flares prevent the corona from heating
The Sun's core is actually cooler than believed
Cosmic rays heat the corona selectively
The corona is an optical illusion during eclipses
Atmospheric convection from below never reaches it
e: This 'coronal heating problem' is one of the biggest mysteries in solar physics. The corona is hundreds of times hotter than the surface below it, which seems to violate common sense about heat flowing away from a source.

s: There may be a rogue planet — a planet not orbiting any star — for every star in the Milky Way, meaning billions of worlds drifting alone through darkness.
q: What is a rogue planet?
a: A planet not orbiting any star, drifting through space
d: A planet with an elliptical orbit around two stars
A planet that has been ejected into a star's corona
An artificial spacecraft designed to mimic a planet
A failed star that never ignited hydrogen fusion
A planetary moon orbiting a rogue planet
A comet with a solid rocky core
e: Gravitational interactions can eject planets from their birth systems. Recent surveys suggest these starless wanderers may be incredibly common, possibly outnumbering stars in our galaxy.

s: The Apollo astronauts left retroreflectors on the Moon that scientists still bounce lasers off today to measure the Earth-Moon distance to within millimeters.
q: What did Apollo astronauts leave on the Moon that scientists still use today?
e: These special mirrors reflect light directly back to its source. By measuring the round-trip time of a laser pulse, we know the Moon is moving away from Earth at 3.8 cm per year.

s: A white dwarf star is so dense that a piece the size of a sugar cube would weigh about 1 ton on Earth.
q: What is the approximate weight of a sugar-cube-sized piece of white dwarf material?
e: White dwarfs are the remnants of Sun-like stars that have exhausted their nuclear fuel. They pack roughly the mass of the Sun into an object the size of Earth, supported by electron degeneracy pressure.

s: The Chandrasekhar limit — 1.4 solar masses — is the maximum mass a white dwarf can have before it collapses into a neutron star or explodes as a Type Ia supernova.
q: What is the Chandrasekhar limit?
a: The maximum mass of a white dwarf (about 1.4 solar masses)
d: The minimum mass for a neutron star to form
The threshold for supernova detonation in any star
The mass limit for stable brown dwarfs
The critical density point in stellar cores
The mass required for planet formation
The limit for main-sequence star stability
e: Discovered by Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar at age 19, this limit earned him the 1983 Nobel Prize. Type Ia supernovae from white dwarfs exceeding this limit are used as 'standard candles' to measure cosmic distances.

s: The Oort Cloud, a hypothetical shell of icy objects surrounding our solar system, may extend nearly 2 light-years from the Sun — almost halfway to the nearest star.
q: How far from the Sun might the Oort Cloud extend?
e: Long-period comets that take thousands of years to orbit the Sun are thought to originate from this distant reservoir. No spacecraft has ever reached it, and direct observation remains impossible with current technology.

s: Betelgeuse, the red supergiant star in Orion's shoulder, is so large that if placed at the center of our solar system, it would engulf Mars and possibly Jupiter.
q: How large is the star Betelgeuse compared to our Sun?
e: With a diameter roughly 700 times that of the Sun, Betelgeuse is nearing the end of its life and could explode as a supernova within the next 100,000 years — creating a light show visible in daylight from Earth.

s: The Wow! signal, detected in 1977, was a 72-second radio burst from space that remains one of the strongest candidates for an extraterrestrial signal ever recorded.
q: When was the famous Wow! signal detected?
e: Astronomer Jerry Ehman circled the data printout and wrote 'Wow!' in the margin. Despite decades of follow-up observations, the signal from the direction of Sagittarius has never been detected again.

s: Quasars are the brightest objects in the universe — some are over 1,000 times brighter than the entire Milky Way galaxy despite being smaller than our solar system.
q: What powers the extreme brightness of quasars?
d: Fusion reactions in their cores releasing enormous energy
Reflection of surrounding starlight through cosmic lenses
Stellar collision chains creating cascading explosions
Conversion of dark matter into visible radiation
e: Powered by supermassive black holes devouring matter at enormous rates, quasars can outshine trillions of stars. The most distant ones show us the universe as it was billions of years ago.

s: The Cassini spacecraft ended its 13-year mission at Saturn by intentionally plunging into the planet's atmosphere in 2017 — to prevent contaminating potentially habitable moons.
q: Why did NASA crash the Cassini spacecraft into Saturn?
a: To prevent contaminating potentially habitable moons
d: To study Saturn's radiation belts up close
To avoid the radiation damage of extended Saturn observation
To collect atmospheric samples for Earth return
To measure Saturn's magnetic field strength directly
To accelerate the spacecraft for an outer solar system mission
e: NASA chose this dramatic 'Grand Finale' to protect Enceladus and Titan from possible Earth microbe contamination. During its final orbits, Cassini dove between Saturn and its rings, gathering unprecedented data.

s: Proxima Centauri b orbits the nearest star to our Sun and sits within its star's habitable zone.
q: Which exoplanet orbits the closest star to our Sun?
e: Discovered in 2016, Proxima Centauri b is a rocky exoplanet about 4.24 light-years away, orbiting in the zone where liquid water could exist on the surface.

s: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about 6 billion tons on Earth.
q: Approximately how much would a teaspoon of neutron star material weigh on Earth?
e: Neutron stars are so incredibly dense that their matter is compressed to nuclear densities, making even a tiny amount extraordinarily heavy by everyday standards.

s: Europa, one of Jupiter's moons, has a subsurface ocean that may contain more water than all of Earth's oceans combined.
q: Which moon of Jupiter is believed to have a subsurface ocean larger than Earth's oceans?
e: Beneath Europa's icy crust lies a global saltwater ocean estimated to hold two to three times the volume of water found in all of Earth's oceans, making it a prime target for astrobiology.

s: The Mars rover Perseverance carries a small helicopter called Ingenuity, which achieved the first powered flight on another planet.
q: What was the name of the helicopter that achieved the first powered flight on Mars?
e: On April 19, 2021, Ingenuity performed a 39-second hover at about 3 meters altitude, proving that controlled powered flight is possible in Mars's thin atmosphere.

s: The James Webb Space Telescope observes the universe primarily in infrared light, allowing it to see through cosmic dust clouds.
q: What type of light does the James Webb Space Telescope primarily observe?
e: JWST's infrared instruments can detect heat radiation from objects obscured by dust, revealing newborn stars, distant galaxies, and atmospheric details of exoplanets that visible-light telescopes cannot see.

s: Dark matter makes up about 27% of the universe's total mass-energy content, yet it has never been directly detected.
q: Approximately what percentage of the universe's mass-energy is dark matter?
e: Scientists infer dark matter's existence from gravitational effects on visible matter, galaxies, and the large-scale structure of the universe, but its particles remain unidentified.

s: The asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter contains millions of rocky objects, but their total mass is less than 4% of the Moon's mass.
q: The total mass of the asteroid belt is less than what percentage of the Moon's mass?
e: Despite science fiction depictions of dense asteroid fields, the belt is mostly empty space, and all its objects combined would form a body far smaller than our Moon.

s: The International Space Station orbits Earth at about 28,000 km/h, completing one orbit roughly every 90 minutes.
q: Approximately how long does it take the ISS to orbit Earth once?
e: At an altitude of about 400 km, the ISS travels fast enough to circle the entire planet 16 times per day, meaning astronauts witness 16 sunrises and sunsets daily.

s: SpaceX's Falcon 9 rocket was the first orbital-class rocket to successfully land its first stage booster for reuse.
q: Which rocket was the first orbital-class booster to land successfully for reuse?
e: In December 2015, Falcon 9 landed its first stage upright after delivering payloads to orbit, revolutionizing spaceflight economics by proving rockets could be reflown.

s: Solar flares can release energy equivalent to millions of nuclear bombs in just minutes.
q: What solar phenomenon can release energy equivalent to millions of nuclear bombs?
e: These intense bursts of radiation from the Sun's surface are caused by magnetic field reconnection and can disrupt satellite communications, power grids, and GPS systems on Earth.

s: Spiral galaxies like the Milky Way have rotating arms where new stars are actively forming.
q: What type of galaxy has rotating arms where new stars form?
e: The spiral arms are density waves that compress gas and dust as they pass, triggering star formation. The Milky Way has at least four major spiral arms.

s: Gravitational waves were first directly detected in 2015 by LIGO, confirming a prediction Einstein made 100 years earlier.
q: Which observatory first directly detected gravitational waves in 2015?
e: The detection came from two merging black holes about 1.3 billion light-years away. The signal matched Einstein's general relativity predictions precisely, earning the 2017 Nobel Prize in Physics.

s: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, is the most distant human-made object, now traveling in interstellar space.
q: Which spacecraft is the most distant human-made object from Earth?
e: Voyager 1 crossed the heliopause in 2012, entering the region between stars. It continues to send data from over 24 billion km away, taking signals over 22 hours to reach Earth.

s: Red giant stars form when a star exhausts the hydrogen fuel in its core and begins fusing hydrogen in a shell around it.
q: What causes a star to become a red giant?
d: Beginning fusion of helium in the core
Sudden increase in solar wind output
Accumulation of too much mass from stellar wind
Collision with another star in the system
e: As the core contracts and heats up, the outer layers expand dramatically, cooling the surface to a reddish color. Our Sun will become a red giant in about 5 billion years.

s: The cosmic microwave background radiation is the afterglow of the Big Bang, dating to about 380,000 years after the universe began.
q: The cosmic microwave background is the afterglow of what event?
d: The collision of the first galaxies
The formation of the first stars
The ejection of matter from black holes
Stellar nucleosynthesis in the early universe
The ignition of the first quasars
The accretion disk formation around dark matter
e: At that time, the universe cooled enough for atoms to form, allowing photons to travel freely. This radiation now fills all of space at a temperature of about 2.7 Kelvin.

s: Tardigrades (water bears) have survived exposure to the vacuum of space, making them candidates for studying life's resilience beyond Earth.
q: Which microscopic animal has survived exposure to the vacuum of space?
e: In 2007, tardigrades were exposed to open space on the FOTON-M3 mission and many survived, withstanding vacuum, UV radiation, and cosmic rays — feats no other animal has matched.

s: Astronauts on the ISS can lose up to 1-2% of their bone density per month due to microgravity.
q: Approximately how much bone density can astronauts lose per month in microgravity?
e: Without the constant pull of gravity, bones lose calcium and weaken, similar to severe osteoporosis. Astronauts must exercise about two hours daily to slow this bone loss.

s: Titan, Saturn's largest moon, is the only moon in the solar system with a dense atmosphere, and it has lakes of liquid methane.
q: Which moon has a dense atmosphere and lakes of liquid methane?
e: Titan's thick nitrogen atmosphere and surface methane cycle (rain, rivers, lakes) make it eerily Earth-like in structure, though at -179°C the chemistry is radically different.

s: The Hubble Space Telescope has been operating in orbit since 1990 and has made over 1.5 million observations.
q: In what year was the Hubble Space Telescope launched into orbit?
e: Hubble orbits about 547 km above Earth, avoiding atmospheric distortion. Its observations have led to breakthroughs including measuring the universe's expansion rate and discovering supermassive black holes in galaxies.

s: Halley's Comet is visible from Earth roughly every 75-79 years, with its next appearance expected around 2061.
q: Approximately how often is Halley's Comet visible from Earth?
e: This famous periodic comet has been observed for over two millennia. Its orbit takes it from beyond Neptune's orbit to inside Venus's orbit, creating a spectacular tail as it approaches the Sun.

s: Dark energy, which drives the accelerating expansion of the universe, makes up about 68% of the universe's total energy.
q: Approximately what percentage of the universe's energy is dark energy?
e: Discovered through observations of distant supernovae in 1998, dark energy is the dominant component of the universe, yet its fundamental nature remains one of physics' greatest mysteries.

s: Enceladus, a small moon of Saturn, shoots geysers of water ice into space from cracks near its south pole.
q: Which Saturn moon ejects water ice geysers from its south pole?
e: The Cassini spacecraft discovered these plumes in 2005, suggesting a subsurface ocean heated by tidal forces. The plumes contain water vapor, salts, and organic molecules — ingredients for life.
